title: SEGMENT 11 — Target two, the ribosome: setup and the 30S inhibitors

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Target two, the ribosome: setup and the 30S inhibitors
We're moving now to the second great target: the ribosome, the protein-synthesis machine.
This is the busiest category — more drug classes attack the ribosome than any other single target — so I want to set up the machinery carefully first, and then the drugs will sort themselves.
Recall the selective-toxicity logic.
Bacterial ribosomes are 70S, made of a 30S small subunit and a 50S large subunit.
Yours are 80S, made of 40S and 60S.

The numbers, by the way, are sedimentation coefficients — how fast the piece sinks in a centrifuge — which is why 30 plus 50 doesn't equal 70; you can't just add them, because the value depends on shape and density, not just mass.
Don't let the arithmetic bother you.
The point is: the bacterial ribosome is different enough from yours that we can drug it selectively.
Let me remind you what the ribosome actually does, because the drugs map onto specific steps.
The ribosome reads messenger RNA and builds a protein, one amino acid at a time.
Amino acids are delivered by transfer RNAs.

There are key sites on the ribosome: the A site, where a new incoming amino-acid-carrying tRNA docks; the P site, which holds the growing protein chain; and then the chain gets handed from the P-site tRNA to the A-site amino acid, forming a new peptide bond — that bond-forming reaction is catalyzed by an activity called peptidyl transferase, which lives in the large 50S subunit.
After the bond forms, the whole assembly shifts over by one codon — that's translocation — and the cycle repeats.
Docking, bond formation, translocation; docking, bond formation, translocation.
Every antibiotic in this category jams one of those steps, and which step tells you a lot about whether the drug ends up static or cidal.
Now, before we go further, the complication I promised earlier.
Your cytoplasmic ribosomes are 80S — safe.

But you have a second population of ribosomes hiding inside your mitochondria, and for deep evolutionary reasons — mitochondria descend from ancient bacteria — your mitochondrial ribosomes resemble bacterial 70S ribosomes more than they resemble your own 80S ribosomes.
So a drug that targets the bacterial 70S ribosome can sometimes also poke at your mitochondrial ribosomes, and that is the source of a lot of the toxicity we'll see in this category.
So unlike the cell-wall drugs, where the target was utterly absent from humans, here the target has a faint echo inside our own cells.
Bigger overlap, more toxicity.
The selective-toxicity pattern I set up at the start is already paying off.
Okay.
Let's split the drugs by which subunit they hit.
We'll do the 30S small-subunit drugs first.
There are two classes that matter: the aminoglycosides and the tetracyclines, and — handy mnemonic — they're both "30S" drugs, so think of the two families that bind the small subunit.
The aminoglycosides — gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin, streptomycin.
These are fascinating because they're the great exception to a rule I gave you earlier.
Most protein-synthesis inhibitors are bacteriostatic — and you'd expect that, because if you just gently stop the ribosome and then the drug washes out, the ribosome resumes and the bug recovers.
But aminoglycosides are strongly bactericidal.
Why?
Their mechanism isn't gentle.

They bind to the 30S subunit, specifically to the 16S ribosomal RNA right at the decoding region, and they do two destructive things: they block proper initiation of protein synthesis, and — crucially — they cause the ribosome to misread the genetic code.
The ribosome starts inserting the wrong amino acids.
So the bacterium isn't just making less protein; it's churning out garbled, nonfunctional, misfolded proteins.
Some of those defective proteins jam into the cell membrane and punch holes in it, which lets more drug flood in, which causes more misreading — a vicious cycle that ends in cell death.
It's not a gentle freeze; it's a corruption of the machine that cascades into catastrophe.
That's why they kill.

And remember, aminoglycosides are concentration-dependent killers with a long post-antibiotic effect — that's the pair of properties I flagged earlier — which is exactly why we now give them as one big once-daily dose: a huge peak drives aggressive killing and misreading, and the prolonged post-antibiotic effect covers the trough.
Two more mechanistic points about aminoglycosides that students always find satisfying once they see the logic.
First: aminoglycosides require oxygen to get into the bacterial cell.
Their uptake across the membrane is an active, oxygen-dependent process.
So they do not work against strict anaerobes — bacteria that live without oxygen — because the drug can't get inside.
Again: not a target problem, an access problem.
Second: their toxicity profile.
Aminoglycosides are notorious for damaging the kidneys and the inner ear — nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, the latter sometimes permanent.
And where does that come from?
At least partly from those mitochondrial ribosomes we just discussed; the cells of the inner ear are particularly vulnerable.
So the toxicity traces straight back to the imperfect selectivity of a 70S-targeting drug in a body that hides 70S-like ribosomes in its mitochondria.
Mechanism explains the side effect.
The other 30S class: the tetracyclines — tetracycline itself, doxycycline, minocycline.
Their mechanism is cleaner and gentler.
They also bind the 30S subunit, but they sit in the A site — the docking bay — and physically block the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA from binding.
No tRNA can dock, so no new amino acid gets added, so the protein chain can't grow.
But notice: this is reversible blockade.
Remove the drug, the A site clears, and synthesis resumes.
So tetracyclines are bacteriostatic — they freeze rather than kill — exactly as the gentle-inhibition logic predicts.

Tetracyclines are broad-spectrum and especially useful against unusual intracellular organisms — the bugs that cause things like Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, chlamydia, atypical pneumonias.

A couple of mechanistically-flavored clinical pearls: tetracyclines bind calcium and other divalent metal ions avidly — they chelate them — which is why you shouldn't take them with milk or antacids, because the drug binds the calcium and you don't absorb it, and it's also why they deposit in growing teeth and bone and can stain children's teeth, so we avoid them in young kids and pregnancy.
That side effect is a direct readout of the molecule's chemistry.

The newer drug tigecycline is a tetracycline derivative engineered to dodge some common resistance mechanisms — a nice example of medicinal chemistry chasing resistance, which is our theme for the end of the lecture.
